does: This paper introduces two novel metrics—Knowledge Separability Score (KSS) and Knowledge Persistence Score (KPS)—to evaluate multilingual machine unlearning in large language models. KSS measures overall unlearning quality across multiple languages, while KPS assesses whether information is consistently removed across different language pairs rather than merely persisting in alternate linguistic forms. The authors evaluate existing unlearning methods using these metrics on multilingual corpora, revealing that prior evaluation approaches fail to capture how sensitive information distributes and potentially transfers across languages, and provide empirical insights into multilingual-specific unlearning phenomena.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature in machine unlearning, which addresses the computational challenge of removing specific information from trained models—a capability increasingly important for privacy compliance and commercial LLM deployment. It extends prior work on PII removal and privacy-preserving machine learning by recognizing a fundamental gap: existing per-language unlearning evaluation protocols, borrowed from monolingual settings, cannot detect when information removal in one language allows information leakage through alternative linguistic representations. The paper responds to the empirical reality that modern LLMs are trained on genuinely multilingual data, where knowledge representations can be distributed across language boundaries in ways not captured by isolated per-language metrics.
enables: This work establishes evaluation foundations for more robust multilingual privacy safeguards in commercial LLM systems. By providing metrics that detect cross-linguistic information persistence, it enables developers to identify failure modes where unlearning appears successful in isolated languages but fails at the system level. Future work can build on KSS and KPS to develop unlearning methods specifically designed for multilingual settings, potentially using language-aware regularization or joint optimization across language pairs. The framework also opens investigation into how knowledge distributes across typologically different languages and script systems, which could inform more fundamental understanding of how LLMs encode and organize multilingual information.
